title: Network Security and Firewall Policy
content:
Network Security and Firewall Policy v4.0 — Aligned with NIST SP 800-41, Cisco SAFE Architecture, and DoD STIG Network guidelines.

1. NETWORK SEGMENTATION
1.1 The enterprise network must be segmented into security zones: DMZ, Internal Production, Development/Test, Management, and Guest.
1.2 Each zone must be separated by firewalls or layer-3 access control lists enforcing least-privilege traffic rules.
1.3 Flat networks are prohibited. Microsegmentation must be implemented for workloads handling sensitive data (PII, PHI, financial records).
1.4 East-west traffic between segments must be inspected and filtered, not just north-south perimeter traffic.
1.5 IoT and OT devices must reside on dedicated, isolated VLANs with no direct access to production data networks.

2. FIREWALL CONFIGURATION AND MANAGEMENT
2.1 All firewalls must operate on a default-deny (implicit deny) policy. Only explicitly authorized traffic is permitted.
2.2 Firewall rules must be documented with: business justification, requestor, approver, source/destination, ports/protocols, and expiration date.
2.3 Overly permissive rules (ANY-ANY, broad subnet ranges) are prohibited and must be remediated within 30 days of detection.
2.4 Firewall rules must be reviewed quarterly by the network security team. Unused rules must be removed within 14 days of identification.
2.5 Changes to firewall rules require change management approval (CAB) and must follow the Change Management Policy (OPS-003).
2.6 Emergency firewall changes require post-implementation review within 48 hours.

3. DISABLING OR BYPASSING FIREWALLS
3.1 Disabling a firewall on any production system is strictly prohibited without written CISO approval and a documented compensating control plan.
3.2 Host-based firewalls (Windows Defender Firewall, iptables, firewalld) must remain enabled on all endpoints and servers.
3.3 Temporarily disabling a host firewall for troubleshooting requires a security exception ticket, is limited to 1 hour maximum, and must be supervised by a security engineer.
3.4 Any unauthorized firewall disabling is a Critical security incident requiring immediate SOC notification.

4. INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
4.1 Network IDS/IPS must be deployed at all network zone boundaries and internet egress points.
4.2 IDS/IPS signatures must be updated at least daily.
4.3 All IDS/IPS alerts classified as High or Critical must be investigated by the SOC within 1 hour.
4.4 Network traffic anomaly detection (NetFlow/IPFIX analysis) must be implemented for baseline deviation alerting.

5. REMOTE ACCESS AND VPN
5.1 Remote access to internal resources must use an approved VPN solution with AES-256 encryption and TLS 1.2+.
5.2 Split tunneling is prohibited for connections to production and sensitive environments.
5.3 VPN access requires MFA per SEC-002 Multi-Factor Authentication Policy.
5.4 VPN sessions must time out after 12 hours of continuous connection or 30 minutes of inactivity.
5.5 All VPN connections must be logged with source IP, user identity, connection duration, and bytes transferred.

6. WIRELESS NETWORK SECURITY
6.1 Enterprise Wi-Fi must use WPA3-Enterprise with 802.1X (EAP-TLS) authentication.
6.2 Guest Wi-Fi must be completely isolated from internal networks with bandwidth throttling.
6.3 Rogue access point detection must be enabled and monitored continuously.
6.4 WEP and WPA (original) are prohibited on all enterprise networks.

7. DNS SECURITY
7.1 All internal DNS queries must be resolved through enterprise-managed DNS servers with DNS Security Extensions (DNSSEC) enabled.
7.2 DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) and DNS-over-TLS (DoT) must be configured for external queries.
7.3 DNS query logging must be enabled and retained for a minimum of 90 days for threat hunting.
7.4 Known malicious domains must be blocked via DNS sinkhole or threat intelligence feed integration.

8. INTERNAL IP ADDRESS AND NETWORK INFORMATION
8.1 Internal IP addressing schemes, subnet maps, network topology diagrams, and VLAN assignments are classified as Confidential.
8.2 Network documentation must not be shared externally or stored in unapproved locations.
8.3 Requests for internal network information must be approved by the network security team lead.
8.4 Automated systems must not expose internal IP addresses in HTTP headers, error messages, or API responses.

9. REFERENCES
- NIST SP 800-41 Rev 1 Guidelines on Firewalls and Firewall Policy
- NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 SC-7 Boundary Protection
- Cisco SAFE Architecture Guide
- DoD STIG Network Firewall V-251516
- CIS Controls v8 Controls 9.1-9.7, 12.1-12.8
- PCI DSS v4.0 Requirement 1
